name: Ayakashi
description: Ayakashi are a class of yōkai, or supernatural entities, in Japanese folklore, primarily associated with the sea and coastal regions. They are often described as shapeshifting spirits that appear above the surface of the water, sometimes as a hazy or shimmering light, and are believed to be the spirits of those who drowned at sea. Ayakashi are considered dangerous and malevolent, often blamed for shipwrecks, capsizing boats, and luring sailors to their deaths. They are said to possess the ability to create phantom ships or mirages, leading seafarers astray. While their exact forms are variable, they are consistently linked to maritime disasters and serve as a cautionary tale against the perils of the sea. In some accounts, they are also described as monstrous sea creatures with immense size and power, capable of generating violent storms and whirlpools. The nature of Ayakashi is deeply intertwined with the respect and fear the Japanese hold for the ocean.
subtitle: Malevolent sea spirits in Japanese folklore
appearance: Often described as shimmering lights above the water, phantom ships, or monstrous sea creatures with varying forms. They are typically associated with a hazy or indistinct presence, making their true appearance difficult to discern.
habitat: Coastal regions, open ocean, particularly in areas known for shipwrecks or dangerous currents.
behavior: Malevolent and deceptive, Ayakashi actively seek to cause harm to humans, particularly sailors and fishermen. They use their abilities to create illusions and storms to disorient and drown their victims. They are driven by a sense of vengeance or resentment towards the living.

keyMyths.title: The Ayakashi of the Shimane Coast
keyMyths.description: Local fishermen in Shimane Prefecture tell tales of a large, glowing Ayakashi that appears before storms, its presence signaling impending danger. Legend says offering sake to the sea can appease the spirit, preventing disaster. Disregarding the warning results in the vessel's inevitable demise, swallowed by the ensuing waves, leaving no trace behind as if the sea never existed.

keyMyths.title: The Phantom Ship of Ise Bay
keyMyths.description: Sailors in Ise Bay speak of a phantom ship, an illusion created by an Ayakashi, that lures unsuspecting vessels onto hidden reefs. The ship vanishes as quickly as it appears, leaving the crew to perish in the treacherous waters. The only salvation lies in recognizing the illusion before it's too late and performing a purification ritual with salt.

keyMyths.title: The Drowned Sailor's Revenge
keyMyths.description: It is said that an Ayakashi is formed from the tormented soul of a sailor who died at sea. Driven by resentment, the Ayakashi seeks to claim the lives of other sailors, trapping them in a cycle of vengeance. Only a Buddhist priest performing a proper memorial service can lay the spirit to rest and prevent further tragedies.
keyMyths.source: Buddhist Temple Records of Coastal Villages

sources.author: Hearn, Lafcadio
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese folk tales and ghost stories, providing insights into the beliefs and superstitions surrounding yōkai, including those related to the sea.
